blocks.text: On the heights, beside the way, at the crossroads she takes her stand;
beside the gates in front of the town, at the entrance of the portals she cries out:
2–3 : , perhaps the elevated administrative section of ancient cities (cf. 9.3,14). , see 1.20–21n.
“To you, O people, I call, and my cry is to all that live.
O simple ones, learn prudence; acquire intelligence, you who lack it.
4–5 : She calls first to everyone ( ) and then, as in 1.22, to the (see 1.4n.) and fools ( ).
Hear, for I will speak noble things, and from my lips will come what is right;
for my mouth will utter truth; wickedness is an abomination to my lips.
7 : (i.e., something loathed); see 3.32n.
All the words of my mouth are righteous; there is nothing twisted or crooked in them.
They are all straight to one who understands and right to those who find knowledge.
Take my instruction instead of silver, and knowledge rather than choice gold;
for wisdom is better than jewels, and all that you may desire cannot compare with her.
6–11 : The integrity of Wisdom's speech.
I, wisdom, live with prudence, and I attain knowledge and discretion.
The fear of the Lord is hatred of evil. Pride and arrogance and the way of evil and perverted speech I hate.
13 : , see. 1.7n. Her affections mirror God's.
I have good advice and sound wisdom; I have insight, I have strength.
12–14 : Wisdom's character.
By me kings reign, and rulers decree what is just;
by me rulers rule, and nobles, all who govern rightly.
15–16 : Wisdom inspires all just government; see 16.12–13n.; cf. Solomon's prayer for wisdom (1 Kings 3.6–15).
I love those who love me, and those who seek me diligently find me.
17 : , she reciprocates affection and is accessible to those who search for her (in contrast, see Job 28).
Riches and honor are with me, enduring wealth and prosperity.
My fruit is better than gold, even fine gold, and my yield than choice silver.
I walk in the way of righteousness, along the paths of justice,
endowing with wealth those who love me, and filling their treasuries.
18–21 : Wisdom's benefits.
The Lord created me at the beginning of his work, the first of his acts of long ago.

22 : The meaning of the Heb verb (“qanah”) translated is debated. Usually translated “to acquire” (see 4.5,7n.), it can also mean “to create,” particularly when God is the subject (e.g., Gen 14.19; Deut 32.6; Ps 139.13).
Ages ago I was set up, at the first, before the beginning of the earth.
23 : is likewise contested, perhaps “poured out” (Heb “nasak”), as with the anointing of a king (see Ps 2.6), or “woven, formed” (Heb “sakak”), as in knit together (see Ps 139.13; Job 10.11).
When there were no depths I was brought forth, when there were no springs abounding with water.
24 : , i.e., the primeval ocean (Gen 1.2).
Before the mountains had been shaped, before the hills, I was brought forth—
when he had not yet made earth and fields,a or the world's first bits of soil.
When he established the heavens, I was there, when he drew a circle on the face of the deep,
27 : , i.e., the horizon.
when he made firm the skies above, when he established the fountains of the deep,
when he assigned to the sea its limit, so that the waters might not transgress his command, when he marked out the foundations of the earth,
then I was beside him, like a master worker; and I was daily his delight, rejoicing before him always,
30 : (Heb “ʾamon”) is also unclear. Alternative translations are “faithful one” and “nurtured one, child.”
rejoicing in his inhabited world and delighting in the human race.
22–31 : Wisdom, God, and creation (see 3.19–20). Wisdom is clear that she was with God at creation, but the exact nature of their relationship and her role is uncertain.
“And now, my children, listen to me: happy are those who keep my ways.
Hear instruction and be wise, and do not neglect it.
Happy is the one who listens to me, watching daily at my gates, waiting beside my doors.
34 : , where a lover waits expectantly for his beloved in ancient Near Eastern love poetry (e.g., Song 2.9; Sir 14.20,22–25).
For whoever finds me finds life and obtains favor from the Lord;
but those who miss me injure themselves; all who hate me love death.”
1.1–9.18: The proverbs of Solomon son of David, king of Israel.
8.1–36: Wisdom's self-revelation. The most developed poem about Wisdom in chs 1–9 (see 1.20–33; 3.13–18; 4.5–9; 7.4–5). Compare Sir 24.1–22; Wis 7.7–10.18.
